risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The paper states that 40 patients were studied with 20 in each group, but it does not describe how participants were allocated to saline versus milrinone. There is no explicit mention of a random method (e.g., computer-generated sequence, random numbers, blocks, or central randomization), so the adequacy of sequence generation cannot be judged from the report. Comment: Allocation is reported, but the method of generating the allocation sequence is not.

Summary: The paper states that patients received either saline (n=20) or milrinone (n=20) but provides no description of how the next assignment was concealed from the person enrolling participants. It mentions that "all patients were evaluated before giving them anesthetic" and that the surgeon/anesthesiologist were blinded, but does not describe central allocation, opaque sealed envelopes, identical containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge of assignment. Comment: Randomization/assignment is described without any explicit allocation concealment method, so concealment cannot be judged as low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After performing sternotomy, patients received saline (n=20, controls) or milrinone (n=20, Sanofi, Korea) 0.5 µg/kg/min during coronary artery anastomosis." and "All procedures were performed by the same cardiac surgeon and anesthesiologist blinded to the treatment groups." Comment: The report explicitly states that the surgeon and anesthesiologist were blinded, but it does not state that the participants were blinded. Because this was an active infusion versus saline during surgery, personnel may have been blinded, but participants under anesthesia could not meaningfully influence intraoperative delivery and postoperative care may still have been affected by allocation knowledge if blinding was incomplete. Since the study does not clearly confirm participant blinding or full placebo blinding, and the outcomes include hemodynamic measures and postoperative complications that could be influenced by knowledge of treatment, the safest overall judgement is high risk.

Quote: "All procedures were performed by the same cardiac surgeon and anesthesiologist blinded to the treatment groups." Comment: The main outcomes were hemodynamic variables and RVEF measured by a computerized pulmonary artery catheter/monitoring system, plus 30-day morbidity outcomes (mortality, myocardial infarction, cerebral infarction, renal failure, mechanical ventilation >48 hours, re-operation, infections) recorded from clinical follow-up. These are largely objective outcomes, and the study explicitly states that the surgeon and anesthesiologist were blinded to treatment groups. There is no indication that subjective clinician-rated or patient-reported outcomes were assessed, so knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have biased outcome measurement.

Summary: The paper indicates the trial enrolled 40 patients (20 per group) and reports postoperative/30-day outcomes for both groups, but it does not describe any dropouts, exclusions, or missing outcome data; the Results state that there were no early deaths and similar complication rates, implying complete or near-complete follow-up for the analysed outcomes. Comment: Because the report provides no evidence of loss to follow-up or missing data at the relevant time points, and the planned sample appears to have been retained for analysis, attrition bias is unlikely.
